items.title: Double-entry accounting is mandatory
items.description: Every financial transaction must record at least one debit and one credit line, posted to ledger accounts, such that total debits equal total credits. The general ledger is the system of record; sub-ledgers (AR, AP, inventory) roll up to control accounts in the GL.
items.applicability: Any generated ERP module that posts financial impact: invoices, payments, journal entries, stock valuation, payroll.
items.example: A sales invoice of 1000 posts: Debit Accounts Receivable 1000; Credit Sales Revenue 850; Credit Output Tax 150. Total debit 1000 = total credit 1000.
items.limitation: Does not by itself enforce correct account selection; a balanced voucher can still be posted to the wrong accounts. Account mapping rules must be validated separately.

items.title: Reject unbalanced vouchers before posting
items.description: A voucher (journal entry, invoice, payment) must be validated at save/submit time so that the sum of debit amounts equals the sum of credit amounts within a defined rounding tolerance. Posting must be blocked, not warned, when the voucher is out of balance.
items.applicability: Validation layer of any posting document in a generated ERP accounting module.
items.example: On submit, compute sum(debits) - sum(credits); if abs(diff) > tolerance (e.g. 0.005 for 2-dp currency) reject with a domain error and do not write ledger rows.
items.limitation: Tolerance must match the currency's smallest unit; an over-wide tolerance silently absorbs real errors. Multi-currency vouchers require base-currency conversion before the balance check.

items.title: Posted documents are immutable
items.description: Once a financial document is posted (submitted to the ledger), its accounting lines must not be edited or deleted in place. The document carries a status (Draft, Submitted, Cancelled) and only Draft documents are mutable. This preserves the integrity of the audit trail and trial balance.
items.applicability: Document lifecycle for invoices, payments, journal entries, stock entries, and any voucher that posts ledger impact.
items.example: A submitted sales invoice exposes no Edit action; correcting it requires a credit note (reversal) plus a new corrected invoice.
items.limitation: Non-accounting metadata (e.g. a remarks field, attached files) may be amendable by policy; the rule applies strictly to fields that affect ledger or stock impact.

items.title: Correct by reversal, never by editing posted entries
items.description: To correct a posted financial document, create a reversing entry (cancellation, credit note, debit note) that negates the original ledger impact, then post a fresh corrected document. The original and its reversal both remain visible, giving a complete history.
items.applicability: Error-correction and cancellation flows in generated accounting and inventory modules.
items.example: Cancelling a posted purchase invoice generates an automatic reversal voucher (mirror debits/credits) rather than deleting the original GL rows.
items.limitation: Reversal must use the original posting date period if that period is still open, or the current open period otherwise; posting a reversal into a closed period requires period-reopen controls.

items.title: AR and AP control accounts reconcile to sub-ledgers
items.description: Accounts Receivable and Accounts Payable are control accounts in the GL whose balance must always equal the sum of open balances in the customer/supplier sub-ledger. Postings to AR/AP must always carry a party (customer or supplier) reference so the sub-ledger stays reconcilable.
items.applicability: Account schema and posting rules for customer- and supplier-facing documents.
items.example: Every line posted to the Debtors (AR) control account stores the CustomerId; a reconciliation query SUMs the AR ledger by customer and compares the total to the GL control-account balance, expecting zero variance.
items.limitation: Direct journal entries to a control account without a party reference break reconciliation; such postings must be blocked or routed through party-aware documents.

items.title: Fiscal year and accounting period governance
items.description: An ERP must define fiscal years split into accounting periods. Each posting carries a posting date that maps to exactly one period. Periods can be Open or Closed; postings to a Closed period are rejected. Year-end closing rolls P&L balances into retained earnings and opens the next year.
items.applicability: Posting validation, reporting boundaries, and year-end close in a generated accounting module.
items.example: A journal entry dated 2026-03-31 posts to FY2025-26 period 12; if that period is closed, the post is rejected and the user is directed to the current open period.
items.limitation: Calendar layouts vary by jurisdiction (April-March, July-June, fiscal 4-4-5); the period definition must be configurable rather than hardcoded to a calendar year.

items.title: P&L versus Balance Sheet account separation
items.description: Chart-of-accounts entries are typed as either Balance Sheet (assets, liabilities, equity — balances carry forward across years) or Profit & Loss (income, expense — balances reset each year into retained earnings). Reports and year-end close depend on this classification being correct.
items.applicability: Chart-of-accounts design and financial reporting in a generated ERP.
items.example: Sales Revenue is a P&L income account that zeroes at year-end; Accounts Receivable is a Balance Sheet asset that carries its balance into the next fiscal year.
items.limitation: Misclassifying an account corrupts both the P&L and the carried-forward Balance Sheet; classification should be set at account creation and not changed once postings exist.

items.title: Stock ledger is the system of record for quantities
items.description: Every inventory movement (receipt, issue, transfer, adjustment) must append an immutable stock-ledger entry recording item, warehouse, quantity change, valuation rate, and resulting balance. On-hand quantity is derived from the stock ledger, never stored as a single mutable counter that is incremented in place.
items.applicability: Inventory and warehouse modules in a generated ERP.
items.example: A goods receipt of 10 units writes a +10 stock-ledger row; a delivery of 4 writes a -4 row; current on-hand is the running balance (6), reproducible by replaying the ledger.
items.limitation: Deriving on-hand by summing the full ledger on every read does not scale; production systems cache a per-item/warehouse running balance that must be recomputed if back-dated entries are inserted.

items.title: Moving-average valuation method
items.description: Under moving-average valuation, each inbound receipt recomputes the item's average cost as (existing value + inbound value) / (existing qty + inbound qty). Outbound issues are valued at the current moving average. This keeps inventory valuation continuous and avoids per-lot tracking.
items.applicability: Valuation logic for inventory items that do not require FIFO or batch-specific costing.
items.example: On hand 10 @ 5.00 (value 50). Receive 10 @ 7.00 (value 70). New avg = (50+70)/(10+10) = 6.00. A subsequent issue of 5 units is costed at 6.00 = 30.
items.limitation: Moving average smooths cost spikes and is unsuitable where FIFO, LIFO, or batch/serial-specific costing is legally required; the valuation method must be selectable per item or item group.

items.title: Negative-stock guard on outbound movements
items.description: Unless explicitly permitted by configuration, an outbound stock movement must be rejected if it would drive the item's balance in that warehouse below zero. The check uses the current running balance at the movement's posting time, accounting for back-dated entries.
items.applicability: Delivery, material issue, and stock-transfer validation in a generated inventory module.
items.example: On-hand 3 in WH-A; a delivery of 5 is rejected with 'insufficient stock' unless allow-negative-stock is enabled for that item/warehouse.
items.limitation: Allowing negative stock (e.g. for retroactive receipts) is a deliberate configuration; when enabled, valuation can produce unusual average rates that downstream reports must tolerate.

items.title: Stock adjustment via reconciliation entry
items.description: Physical-count corrections are recorded as a stock reconciliation document that sets the counted quantity and value for items in a warehouse and posts the difference (gain or loss) to a stock-adjustment GL account, keeping the stock ledger and the general ledger in agreement.
items.applicability: Cycle-count and physical-inventory correction flows.
items.example: Counted 8 units but system shows 10; the reconciliation posts a -2 stock-ledger row and a corresponding loss to the Stock Adjustment expense account.
items.limitation: Reconciliation must post valuation impact to the GL; a quantity-only adjustment that skips the GL leaves inventory value and the control account out of sync.

items.title: Workflow approval state machine
items.description: Documents requiring approval move through an explicit state machine (e.g. Draft -> Pending Approval -> Approved/Rejected) with defined transitions, allowed roles per transition, and side effects (such as posting on Approved). State and transitions are data-driven so new workflows do not require code changes.
items.applicability: Any generated document type that must be approved before it takes financial or operational effect.
items.example: A purchase order in Pending Approval can only transition to Approved by a user holding the Purchase Manager role; the Approved transition triggers the commitment posting.
items.limitation: Free-form transition rules can create unreachable or deadlocked states; the workflow definition must be validated for at least one path to a terminal state.

items.title: Maker-checker segregation of duties
items.description: The user who creates or submits a sensitive document (the maker) must not be the user who approves it (the checker). The system records both identities and rejects self-approval, enforcing the four-eyes principle for financial and high-risk actions.
items.applicability: Approval flows for payments, journal entries, master-data changes, and other controlled documents.
items.example: User A submits a vendor payment; when User A attempts to approve it, the action is rejected because maker == checker. User B (a different authorised approver) must approve.
items.limitation: Small teams may lack a second approver; the system needs a configurable exception path with heightened logging rather than silently disabling the control.

items.title: Approval matrix by document type and role
items.description: An approval matrix maps each document type to the sequence of roles (or specific approvers) that must approve, optionally in defined order. The matrix is configuration, allowing approval routing to change without code changes.
items.applicability: Routing of approvals across multiple document types in a generated ERP.
items.example: Purchase Order: Supervisor then Finance Manager; Journal Entry: Accountant then Controller. The engine reads the matrix to build the approval steps for each new document.
items.limitation: Order-dependent matrices must handle approver absence (delegation/escalation); without it, a missing approver stalls the document indefinitely.

items.title: Threshold-based approval escalation
items.description: Approval requirements scale with the document's monetary value via configured thresholds. Amounts below a floor may auto-approve; higher bands require additional or more senior approvers. Thresholds are evaluated against the document's base-currency amount.
items.applicability: Value-sensitive approvals such as payments, purchase orders, and expense claims.
items.example: Payment < 1000 auto-approved; 1000-10000 needs Finance Manager; > 10000 needs Finance Manager and Director.
items.limitation: Thresholds must compare a single normalised base-currency amount; comparing raw foreign-currency totals lets users split or denominate amounts to bypass a band.

items.title: Append-only audit trail for controlled actions
items.description: Create, submit, approve, cancel, and master-data changes must write an append-only audit record capturing who, what, when, before/after values, and the originating document. Audit rows are never updated or deleted by application code.
items.applicability: All state-changing operations on financial and master-data entities in a generated ERP.
items.example: Approving a payment writes an audit row: actor, action=Approve, entity=Payment#123, timestamp, prior status, new status, and the maker's identity.
items.limitation: Audit volume grows quickly; retention and archival policy must be defined, and the audit store should be write-restricted at the database level to resist tampering by the app account.

items.title: Role-based access control on every action
items.description: Access is granted through roles mapped to permissions on document types and actions (read, create, submit, approve, cancel, delete). Authorization is enforced server-side on every controller action, not merely by hiding UI elements. Deny-by-default is the baseline.
items.applicability: Authorization layer of every generated ERP module.
items.example: An Accountant role can create and submit journal entries but cannot approve them; the approve endpoint checks the permission server-side and returns 403 if absent.
items.limitation: UI-only hiding is not security; without server-side checks the action is reachable by direct API call. Permission caching must be invalidated when roles change.

items.title: Naming series for document numbering
items.description: Each document type uses a configurable naming series (prefix + zero-padded sequence, optionally with fiscal-year segments) that produces unique, gapless, human-readable identifiers. Number allocation must be concurrency-safe so two simultaneous submits never reuse a number.
items.applicability: Identifier generation for invoices, orders, vouchers, and other documents in a generated ERP.
items.example: Series SINV-2026-#### yields SINV-2026-0001, SINV-2026-0002. The next number is allocated under a transaction/lock to avoid duplicates under concurrency.
items.limitation: Strict gapless numbering conflicts with high-concurrency throughput; if regulatory gaplessness is required, allocation must serialise, which can become a contention point under load.

items.title: Multi-company data isolation
items.description: When the ERP hosts more than one legal entity, every transactional and most master records carry a company reference. Each company has its own chart of accounts and ledgers; queries and reports are always scoped by company, and inter-company transactions post matched entries in both companies.
items.applicability: Data model and query scoping for ERPs supporting multiple companies.
items.example: A report request always filters by CompanyId; a stock transfer between two companies posts a sale in one and a purchase in the other, with an inter-company reconciliation account.
items.limitation: Cross-company queries that forget the company filter leak data between entities; the company scope should be enforced centrally (e.g. a query filter) rather than relied upon per-query.

items.title: Multi-warehouse stock segregation
items.description: Inventory balances and valuation are tracked per item per warehouse. Movements always specify a source and/or target warehouse, and on-hand checks are warehouse-specific. Transfers between warehouses are a paired out/in movement that conserves total quantity.
items.applicability: Inventory modules that operate across multiple physical or logical locations.
items.example: Item A: 10 in WH-Main, 0 in WH-Shop. A transfer of 4 writes -4 in WH-Main and +4 in WH-Shop; total across warehouses stays 10.
items.limitation: In-transit stock between warehouses needs an interim location or status; a single out/in pair without an in-transit concept temporarily hides goods that have left but not arrived.

items.title: Tax templates compute and post tax separately
items.description: Sales and purchase tax is driven by configurable tax templates (rate, account, inclusive/exclusive, compounding order). Tax is computed per line or per document according to the template and posted to dedicated tax-payable/receivable accounts, separate from the net amount.
items.applicability: Tax calculation and posting in generated sales, purchase, and POS modules.
items.example: An exclusive 15% VAT template on a net 100 line posts net 100 to revenue and 15 to Output VAT Payable; the customer is invoiced 115.
items.limitation: Tax rules vary widely by jurisdiction (compounding, reverse charge, withholding, exemptions); templates must be data-driven and validated against the target jurisdiction rather than hardcoded.

items.title: Money stored as fixed-precision decimal, never float
items.description: Monetary and quantity values must use a fixed-precision decimal type (e.g. SQL decimal(18,4), .NET decimal), never binary floating point. Rounding is applied explicitly at defined points (per line, per document total) using a documented rounding mode to keep totals reproducible.
items.applicability: Data types and arithmetic throughout any generated ERP that handles money or valuation.
items.example: A line amount column is decimal(18,4); the document grand total is rounded to the currency's 2 decimal places with banker's or half-up rounding, applied once and stored.
items.limitation: Decimal precision must be consistent across columns and across database providers; mismatched scales cause silent truncation, and aggregation across providers may need explicit materialisation to avoid precision loss.
